summary: Hannes Mühleisen describes how DuckDB was created at CWI in Amsterdam as an in-process analytical database, born from frustration that academic database research wasn't having real-world impact. He explains how talking to practitioners revealed that end-to-end experience mattered more than raw algorithm speed, and how restrictive IT environments shaped DuckDB's zero-dependency design. The conversation covers how Jordan Tigani discovered DuckDB and founded MotherDuck to commercialize it, with Hannes noting that Jordan's credibility from BigQuery validated the scale-up approach they'd been ridiculed for. Hannes reflects on the transition from academic researcher to running DuckDB Labs.
key_points: DuckDB was born from the observation that great academic database ideas weren't having real-world impact because practitioners cared most about the end-to-end data management experience
Working in restrictive academic IT environments with no root access shaped DuckDB's zero-dependency philosophy
VCs wanted them to become Snowflake, but they chose to be technology providers — accepting commercial users' money and running traditionally rather than building a database-as-a-service
Jordan Tigani's BigQuery background validated the scale-up approach — someone from distributed systems saying single-node was serious gave them credibility they lacked
The shift from scale-out to scale-up was initially based on feeling and intuition until real data (like Google's TF data paper showing 95th percentile ML jobs were ~100GB) confirmed it
money_quotes: It seemed insane—two people writing a database management system when these typically require hundreds of people over ten years. But I tend to leap without looking sometimes.
I developed an uncanny ability to run things without Docker and a deep hatred for dependencies.
VCs wanted us to become a Snowflake, but we didn't want that. We wanted to be technology providers, staying open and flexible.
We'd been ridiculed for not taking distributed systems seriously, yet someone with his credentials validated our approach. That was shocking.
This transformation from scale-out to scale-up was surprising because our scale-up idea was based on feeling. We disliked systems like Spark based on intuition. We lacked data.
It is really shocking to see what is possible on laptops. It's something that we have kind of forgotten about.
There's also a big difference between what the cycle costs and what you are paying for the cycle.
key_insight: DuckDB succeeded by focusing on the end-to-end data experience rather than raw query speed, and its scale-up philosophy — initially dismissed by the industry — was validated when practitioners with big-data credentials confirmed that most real-world data isn't actually big.
